title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot visits a local shop in Broadchurch to purchase writing paper and envelopes from Mrs. Sweetman. During their conversation, they discuss the death of Mrs. McKinty. Mrs. Sweetman expresses doubt regarding the guilt of James Bentley, the man accused of the murder, describing him as a shy and non-dangerous individual. She suggests that a tramp might have entered through an open window, though she questions why a thief would leave money behind.

Mrs. Sweetman and her assistant, Edna, provide details regarding Mrs. McKinty’s routine. Edna observes that on the Monday before her death, Mrs. McKinty appeared unusually excited or cheerful. Mrs. Sweetman notes that Mrs. McKinty had purchased a bottle of ink that day, presumably to write a letter. Poirot learns that Mrs. McKinty worked for various residents, including the Samoys at the Long Grass Inn, Dr. Rundle, the Upwards at The Manor, the Wetherbys, and the Carpenters.

Reflecting on the encounter, Poirot realizes that because Mrs. McKinty rarely wrote letters, her purchase of ink is a significant clue, and he seeks to determine her intended recipient. He subsequently questions Bessie Burch, Mrs. McKinty’s niece. Bessie denies receiving any letters from her aunt and explains that Mrs. McKinty found writing difficult, preferring to communicate via telephone through a neighbor, Mr. Benson, the pharmacist. Bessie also notes that her aunt was a very thrifty woman.
